data.title: Grocery Outlet outlines up to 33 new stores and $0.55 EPS target for 2026 as store closures and promotional investments reshape outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Grocery Outlet Holding Corp. (GO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jason Potter, President, CEO & Director, described Q4 results as "unacceptable" and stated, "our outlook for 2026 reflects a business that has more work to do than we expected. I own this and own fixing the issues." He pointed to comp performance deterioration beginning in late September, noting, "basket pressure intensified, resulting in a negative comp for Q4. Comp sales continued to decelerate in January, driven by declining units per transactions and slowing traffic growth." Potter outlined three drivers of comp deceleration: intensified consumer pressure, eroded value perception, and supply chain constraints impacting opportunistic product delivery.
  • Potter announced a focus on restoring the opportunistic product mix, stating, "Over the next several months, our team is intensely focused on ensuring we have the right weight and depth of quality opportunistic branded product flowing into our mix to restore a winning position on value with our customer." He also highlighted new leadership, including Matt Delly unifying merchandising and purchasing, and described a 200 basis point increase in opportunistic sales mix and a 150 basis point increase in shipment volume recently.
  • The CEO detailed a store refresh program targeting 150 stores by year-end, investments in operator tools, and the closure of 36 underperforming stores—mostly in the East—anticipating an "annualized adjusted EBITDA improvement of roughly $12 million." He stated, "These closures do not change our long-term view that ample white space remains ahead of us."
  • Potter signaled a more disciplined growth approach: "we continue to plan to open another 30 to 33 net new stores in 2026, but they do reflect a more disciplined approach." He also outlined a pilot in Virginia for company-run stores before transitioning to independent operators.
  • Christopher Miller, EVP & CFO, said, "Fourth quarter net sales increased 10.7% to $1.22 billion and included an incremental $82.4 million from a 53rd week in 2025." He explained, "Excluding the extra week, net revenue increased 3.2%, driven by the addition of net new stores, partially offset by an 80 basis point decline in comparable store sales."
  • Miller highlighted, "We incurred $109.8 million of noncash impairment charges for long-lived assets in Q4... and the recognition of $149 million noncash goodwill impairment charge." Net loss was "$218.2 million or negative $2.22 per fully diluted share." Adjusted net income increased 28.8% to $18.7 million or $0.19 per share. Adjusted EBITDA was $68 million for the quarter, up from $57.2 million last year.

Outlook

  • Miller provided 2026 guidance: "For the full year, we expect comp store sales growth to be between negative 2% to flat. For the first quarter, we expect comparable store sales to be between negative 2.5% to negative 1.5%."
  • Grocery Outlet plans to add "between 30 and 33 net new stores for this year, fairly evenly distributed across the quarters."
  • "We expect total net sales for our fiscal 2026 of between $4.6 billion to $4.72 billion."
  • Miller forecasted, "For the full fiscal year, we expect adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $220 million to $235 million," and "we expect full year adjusted EPS to be in the range of $0.45 to $0.55 per fully diluted share."
  • Store closures are expected to "impact top line growth by approximately 2%."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter net sales reached $1.22 billion, with $82.4 million attributed to the 53rd week. Excluding this, net revenue grew 3.2%.
  • Comparable store sales declined by 80 basis points, with a 170 basis point drop in average transaction size partially offset by a 90 basis point increase in traffic.
  • Gross profit increased 11.5% to $361 million, and gross margin expanded 20 basis points year-over-year to 29.7%.
  • SG&A rose 13.6% to $337.1 million, representing 27.7% of sales. Net loss was $218.2 million or negative $2.22 per diluted share. Adjusted net income was $18.7 million or $0.19 per share. Adjusted EBITDA was $68 million, with margin at 5.6%.
  • Year-end cash was $69.6 million, and available revolver capacity was about $175 million. Net cash provided by operating activities increased by $110 million to $222.1 million. CapEx before tenant improvement allowances was $220.3 million.

Q&A

  • Jeremy Hamblin, Craig-Hallum, asked about same-store sales trends and the effect of promotional investments. Miller explained, "in February when we started to invest in doing some promotions, we did see some recovery of about 100 basis points in February and expect that to improve in March."
  • Kylie Cohu, Jefferies, inquired about SNAP benefits. Potter clarified, "February has recovered if that was something just to mention. And yes, it's in the guide."
  • Oliver Chen, TD Cowen, probed the timeline for fixing value perception and the impact of new leadership. Potter said, "restoring that pipeline is a 3- to 6-month piece. The promotions... are basically a bridge in the time period it's going to take."
  • Pedro Gil, Morgan Stanley, questioned promotional investments. Potter responded, "it's a quantum of about $20 million, just to clarify. And what we're doing is we're using fresh products... as a bridge. This is not a permanent part of our P&L."
  • John Heinbockel, Guggenheim, asked about the balance between everyday and opportunistic products. Potter stated, "the majority of our product is opportunistic in our stores and will remain that way... treasure hunt is the main event."
  • Edward Kelly, Wells Fargo, discussed long-term margin structure and store growth strategy. Potter answered, "confident that we're going to be able to expand margins over time. What we're doing right now... is a temporary bridge."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about negative comps, the effectiveness of promotional investments, and the sustainability of the business model, with a slightly negative to neutral tone, focusing on the company's ability to restore value perception and execute on its turnaround plans.
  • Management tone was candid and direct, acknowledging underperformance and providing detailed plans for corrective action. Potter stated, "I take responsibility for that. What I can tell you is that we have a clear understanding of the commercial challenge, and we're taking decisive action."
  • Compared to the previous quarter's more optimistic tone, management in Q4 was more defensive and realistic about challenges, while analysts pressed more pointedly on the timing and effectiveness of strategic changes.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from prior positive comp expectations to negative-to-flat for the upcoming year.
  • Strategic focus moved from scaling store refresh and expanding new stores to a more defensive posture with 36 store closures and a disciplined expansion approach.
  • Management confidence in operational fixes remains, but the tone is more cautious and focused on execution.
  • Analysts shifted from curiosity about new initiatives to concern about negative trends and the effectiveness of planned interventions.
  • Financial outlook was reduced, with full-year adjusted EPS guidance lowered to $0.45–$0.55 from the previous full-year expectation of $0.78–$0.80.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged intensified consumer pressure, deteriorating value perception, and supply chain constraints as key risks.
  • Store closures and impairment charges highlight structural challenges in certain geographies, particularly in the East.
  • The company cited the need to rebuild value perception and opportunistic product flow, with execution risk around restoring sales momentum.
  • Analysts voiced concerns about the competitive promotional environment and the company's ability to pivot quickly enough to restore traffic and basket size.

Final Takeaway

Management at Grocery Outlet has acknowledged significant challenges in Q4 2025, marked by underperformance and negative comps, and is taking decisive action through store closures, promotional investments, and a renewed focus on opportunistic products and value perception. The outlook for 2026 is tempered, with flat to negative comp guidance, targeted cost controls, and a disciplined approach to new store openings, as the company works to restore profitability and rebuild investor confidence through operational improvements and strategic execution.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nvidia shifts production focus to Vera Rubin amid China-bound H200 setback - report
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA) has halted production of its H200 AI chips destined for China due to persistent U.S. and Chinese export controls blocking sales. The chipmaker is now reallocating Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) manufacturing capacity to ramp up output for its next-generation Vera Rubin hardware instead, the Financial Times reported, citing people with knowledge of the matter.

“Instead of waiting in a limbo, Nvidia has to move on to what it can achieve with certainty especially when there’s a shortage of supply for its advanced stuff,” one person with knowledge of the plans told FT. “This could in a way accelerate the Vera Rubin delivery and roll out.”

The ongoing export controls and regulatory guardrails in both the U.S. and China appear to be stalling any significant commercial momentum of H200 chips. Nvidia (NVDA) has already produced around 250,000 H200 chips, and with only limited orders expected under U.S. and Chinese restrictions, existing inventory should be enough to meet demand, a person familiar with the matter said.

China’s President Xi Jinping and President Donald Trump are set to meet at the end of March, fueling speculations that H200 export curbs could be eased. If that happens, Nvidia (NVDA) would need up to three months to ramp up H200 production, though existing inventory could bridge demand in the interim, the people added.

Named after the astronomer Vera Rubin, it is Nvidia’s (NVDA) latest chip architecture designed for large-scale data center AI workloads, including training and inference of advanced models, and is in strong demand from top U.S. tech groups such as OpenAI (OPENAI) and Google (GOOG).

Nvidia (NVDA) and TSMC (TSM) did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

data.title: China reportedly orders top oil refiners to halt diesel and gasoline exports amid Gulf crisis
data.description:
China’s government has reportedly told the country’s largest oil refiners to suspend exports of diesel and gasoline as an escalating conflict in the Persian Gulf disrupts the arrival of crude from one of the world’s largest producing regions.

Officials from the National Development and Reform Commission, the country’s top economic planner, met refinery executives and verbally called for a temporary suspension of refined product shipments that would begin immediately, Bloomberg News reported.

The refiners were asked to stop signing new contracts and to negotiate the cancellation of already-agreed shipments, the report said. An exception was made for jet and bunker fuel held in bonded storage and supplies to Hong Kong and Macau.

According to one Seeking Alpha analyst, it is not a coincidence that China has bet so much on renewables over the last few years and has started to accumulate a huge amount of onshore crude inventories: a record high of 1.20 billion barrels in early January. Considering that China typically imports 5 million oil barrels on a daily basis coming from the Strait of Hormuz, if closed, it would take 240 days (34 weeks) to run out of all the oil inventories. 

"What if the war lasts more than 34 weeks? That’s a major concern, but not only for China, for all the world. If anything, China is less of a problem compared to the European Union and Japan, both oil-dependent economies that don’t produce it… China has many more levers to pull before getting severely hit by this war, and for now it is still well covered."

data.title: ChargePoint outlines AI-driven operational improvements and targets ramp of new products while expanding in Europe
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ChargePoint Holdings, Inc. (CHPT) Q4 2026

Management View

  • CEO Richard Wilmer reported "a strong finish to fiscal 2026," with revenue for Q4 at the high end of guidance at $109 million, highlighting year-over-year growth and record non-GAAP gross margin of 33%. Wilmer emphasized, "Cash utilization from operations was minimal and much better than planned."
  • Wilmer stated, "This performance reinforces our return to growth trend, which we expect to accelerate later this year and into next year as our new products ramp into volume." He pointed to double-digit growth in Europe, increased market share in North America, and operational improvements as key factors.
  • Wilmer introduced new KPIs, noting, "Software-only managed ports are non-ChargePoint hardware ports managed by our software and reflect our software-first strategy." At the end of FY '26, ChargePoint had 1.48 million active users, an 8% year-over-year increase.
  • Wilmer cited a partnership with Ford Pro for fleet solutions and a multiyear agreement with RAW Charging valued at $7.5 million, as well as an expanded relationship with Georgia Power.
  • The CEO highlighted ongoing AI deployment, stating, "Our continued deployment of AI is yielding tangible benefits, which we expect to increase substantially as we move through this year."
  • The company appointed Jaser Faruq as Chief Product and Software Officer to enhance its leadership in electrified transportation and innovation.
  • CFO Mansi Khetani stated, "Revenue for the fourth quarter was $109 million, coming in at the high end of our guidance range, up 3% sequentially and up 7% year-on-year."
  • Khetani noted, "Non-GAAP gross margin continued to remain at a record high of 33%, flat sequentially, and up 3 percentage points year-on-year."

Outlook

  • Khetani provided guidance for Q1 2027, expecting revenue in the range of $90 million to $100 million, reflecting typical Q1 seasonality and a prudent approach given the macro environment. She added, "Our range does encompass a growth scenario year-over-year."
  • Management expects continued growth in Europe, with new products built for both Europe and North America slated to drive future revenue.

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue reached $109 million, with network charging systems contributing $58 million (53% of revenue), subscription revenue at $42 million (39%), and other revenue at $9 million (8%).
  • Subscription margin reached a GAAP record of 64% and even higher on a non-GAAP basis.
  • Non-GAAP adjusted EBITDA loss was $18 million, compared with a loss of $19 million in the prior quarter.
  • Cash position at quarter end was $142 million after a $40 million debt payment. Fiscal 2026 net cash usage was $43 million, down from $133 million in the prior year.

Q&A

  • Colin Rusch, Oppenheimer: Asked about eVTOL and automation opportunities. Wilmer responded, "We haven't focused much on that space yet... the bigger near-term opportunity... is autonomous vehicles."
  • Rusch, Oppenheimer: Inquired about cost opportunities in hardware and pricing. Wilmer explained, "Thus far, we have not pushed any price increases into the market... the opportunity for gross margin improvement... is really hinged on a lot of the new hardware platforms."
  • Mark Delaney, Goldman Sachs: Queried about utilization growth vs. Q1 revenue guidance. Khetani replied, "We're guiding to Q1 based on typical seasonality... about a 5% to 15%-ish reduction in Q1 revenue versus Q4... we're taking a prudent approach given the current macro environment."
  • Delaney, Goldman Sachs: Asked about NEVI and domestic content requirements. Wilmer said, "Obligated funds are not going to be affected by any rule changes around domestic content... we'll have to wait for those rules to get finalized."
  • Christopher Pierce, Needham: Asked about OpEx leverage. Khetani stated, "We expect that this non-GAAP OpEx would remain in that current range in the near term... we should see a reduction over the year as... prototyping costs... start coming down."
  • Ryan Pfingst, B. Riley Securities: Asked about the competitive landscape and market share. Wilmer said, "We're capitalizing on some exits from the market by certain parties."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on cost management, growth drivers, competitive positioning, and the timing of new product contributions. Their tone was generally neutral to slightly cautious, with questions probing for specifics on execution and margin improvement.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing operational progress, innovation, and prudent financial management. During Q&A, responses remained measured and focused on tangible results, with Wilmer expressing optimism about AI impact and new product rollouts.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was consistent, while analysts remained focused on operational execution and market opportunities, with a slightly increased focus on competitive dynamics.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from emphasizing overall growth to a more cautious stance for Q1, citing typical seasonality and macro factors.
  • Strategic focus has expanded to highlight AI-driven operational efficiency, new KPIs, and accelerating Europe growth.
  • Key metrics showed sequential and year-over-year improvement in revenue and gross margin, with a continued focus on cash discipline and reduced cash usage.
  • Analysts sustained interest in inventory, margin expansion, and the contribution of new products, but placed more emphasis on market share gains and competitive exits in the current quarter.
  • Management confidence in reaching positive EBITDA now includes references to tangible AI benefits and leadership additions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited potential impacts from changes in NEVI domestic content requirements, with Wilmer stating, "We'll have to wait for those rules to get finalized before we can assess what, if any, impact it will have on us."
  • Seasonality and macroeconomic uncertainty were referenced as factors behind the conservative Q1 outlook.
  • Inventory reduction and working capital improvements remain ongoing areas of focus, with Khetani noting near-term challenges due to product mix and timing.
  • Analyst concerns centered on sustained revenue growth, gross margin improvement, and the competitive landscape amid market consolidation.

Final Takeaway

ChargePoint closed fiscal 2026 with revenue at the top end of guidance, record non-GAAP gross margins, and disciplined cash usage, underpinned by advances in operational efficiency, AI-driven innovation, and new partnerships. The company is guiding for Q1 2027 revenue of $90 million to $100 million due to seasonality and macroeconomic considerations, while expecting continued growth in Europe and from new product ramps. Strategic priorities include leveraging AI for both operational and product differentiation, expanding software-managed ports, and driving market share through innovation and partnerships. Management remains focused on prudent expense control and positioning ChargePoint for long-term growth and profitability as new offerings come to market and market dynamics evolve.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Endeavour Mining beats top-line estimates; introduces FY26 outlook
data.description:
• Endeavour Mining press release (EDVMF): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.93.
  • Revenue of $1.27B (+35.0% Y/Y) beats by $50M.
  • Q4-2025 production of 298koz at an AISC of $1,648/oz.
  • Adjusted EBITDA of $681m for Q4-2025.
  • FCF of $476m ($1,597/oz produced) for Q4-2025.
  • Net Debt reduced by $574m during FY-2025 to $158m; Net debt / Adj. EBITDA leverage ratio of 0.07x at year end.
  • FY-2026 production guidance of 1,090-1,265koz at AISC of $1,600-1,800/oz.

  • 2026 OUTLOOK

    • The Group has reiterated its FY-2026 production and cost guidance at 1,090-1,265koz gold production at an AISC of $1,600-1,800/oz. More details on individual guidance has been provided in the below sections.

    Table 12: FY-2026 production guidance1

    (All amounts in koz, on a 100% basis) 2026 FULL-YEAR GUIDANCE

    Ity 285 — 330
    Mana 155 — 180
    Sabodala-Massawa 260 — 305
    Lafigué 170 — 195

    Ity2 1,300 — 1,500
    Mana 2,000 — 2,250
    Sabodala-Massawa 1,350 — 1,550
    Lafigué2 1,600 — 1,800
    Corporate G&A   45  
    Group AISC 1,600 — 1,800

data.title: American Eagle Outfitters targets mid-single-digit comp sales growth and $390M–$410M operating profit in 2026 while expanding Aerie and OFFLINE
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Eagle Outfitters (AEO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jay Schottenstein, Executive Chairman and CEO, credited a "deliberate action plan" for igniting growth, improving profitability, and fueling a strong finish to 2025. He highlighted "double-digit sales growth in the fourth quarter ahead of plan," record results during Thanksgiving and holiday season, and solid margin performance. Schottenstein noted that Aerie and OFFLINE delivered "23% comp growth" with broad-based demand, and the company exited the quarter with "record brand awareness" and double-digit customer acquisition.
  • Schottenstein announced the exit from the Quiet Logistics third-party business, stating, "This move keeps our focus and investment dollars on our core brands." He reported total revenue of $1.8 billion for the quarter (up 10%), overall comp sales up 8%, adjusted operating income of $180 million (up 27%), and nearly $240 million in cash with no debt. Share buybacks totaled $256 million and dividends $85 million for the year.
  • Jennifer Foyle, President and Executive Creative Director, stated, "Our commitment to product leadership continues to be a key engine that's driving our business," noting acceleration in demand for Aerie—"strength has been broad-based across all categories"—and a strong performance at OFFLINE, which had "steady sales in active bottoms and double-digit growth in sports bras, tops and fashion bottoms." Foyle underscored growing brand awareness and new customer growth at Aerie, and discussed new initiatives to expand product categories and marketing campaigns.
  • Mike Mathias, CFO, stated, "Our strong performance in the fourth quarter is a testament to this work with results coming in ahead of expectations across margins and profitability." Mathias reported consolidated revenue of $1.8 billion (up 10%), comparable sales growth of 8% (Aerie up 23%, AE up 2%), and gross profit of $651 million. He cited $85 million in restructuring charges related to the discontinuation of Quiet Logistics, store impairments, and corporate restructuring, with expected annual savings of $20 million.

Outlook

  • Mathias provided guidance for Q1 2026: "We expect comparable sales growth in the high single digits, with American Eagle comps in the positive low single digits and Aerie OFFLINE comps in the double digits." Operating income for Q1 is expected in the range of $20 million to $25 million, impacted by approximately $30 million in tariff headwinds and increased advertising.
  • For full-year 2026, the company expects operating profit in the range of $390 million to $410 million based on consolidated comparable sales growth in the mid-single digits. Mathias stated, "Guidance reflects the incremental tariffs that were put in place in 2025, which primarily impacts the first half of the year."
  • 80% of annual operating profit is expected to be generated in the second half of the year, due to tariff and advertising spend timing.

Financial Results

  • The company delivered consolidated revenue of $1.8 billion in Q4 2025, with overall comp sales up 8%. Adjusted operating income was $180 million, which exceeded the previous guidance of $167 million to $170 million. Gross profit was $651 million. The adjusted operating margin rose to 10.2% from 8.9% the prior year.
  • Restructuring charges in the quarter totaled approximately $85 million, primarily related to the exit from Quiet Logistics and corporate restructuring, with expected net annual savings of $20 million.
  • Aerie and OFFLINE achieved 23% comp growth, while American Eagle saw 2% comp growth. New Aerie customers grew 14%, and brand awareness climbed 12% year-over-year.
  • Inventory cost increased 10% with units up 3%, reflecting the impact of tariffs. Year-end cash was $239 million, total liquidity around $930 million.

Q&A

  • Paul Lejuez, Citi: Asked about gross margin expectations beyond Q1 and promotional outlook. Mathias responded that gross margin is expected "sort of in that mid- to high 30% range in the first quarter, a little lower than that in the second quarter," with improvement in the second half as tariffs are cycled. Foyle added, "We do expect some pressure in denim...but also get into new categories that are trending."
  • Jay Sole, UBS: Inquired about store openings, Middle East business, and Quiet Logistics impact. Mathias stated, "We're looking at 35 to 40 openings for Aerie and OFFLINE this year" and 25 to 35 net closings for AE. The Quiet Logistics exit is expected to result in about $20 million of annual savings.
  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan: Asked about Aerie’s performance drivers and expense leverage. Foyle explained, "All categories really worked" and noted continued momentum into Q1. Mathias emphasized a "strategic increase in elevation of our advertising spend" in the first half and plans to leverage SG&A in the back half.
  • Jungwon Kim, TD Cowen: Asked about AE brand positioning and intimates performance. Foyle cited ongoing fleet optimization, product innovation, and new talent. Intimates "saw a great success in Q4, and we're continuing that momentum into Q1."
  • Dana Telsey, Telsey Advisory Group: Questioned advertising strategy and store remodel productivity. Mathias said, "We'll do at least around 60, maybe a few more...this year," with remodeled stores outperforming the chain average.
  • Janine Stichter, BTIG: Probed on pricing in response to tariffs. Mathias replied, "No specific intent around tariff pass-through."
  • Multiple analysts followed up on tariff impact, capital allocation, denim trends, and Aerie customer acquisition, with management highlighting cost control, flexibility in product strategy, and ongoing category expansion.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns around gross margin pressure, promotional intensity in key categories (notably denim), tariff impact, and sustainability of recent growth. The tone was neutral to slightly positive, with a focus on understanding the durability of comps and margin drivers.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing operational improvements, product momentum, and strong cash flow. In Q&A, responses were measured, with Mathias at times deferring specifics until more data is available. Statements like "we feel really good about where we're headed" and "I'm confident that we have only just begun to scratch the surface of this brand's massive and long-term potential" (Foyle) signaled confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management tone showed more conviction in the turnaround, with less focus on inventory risk and more on expansion and margin management.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from cautious optimism in Q3 to a more assertive outlook for mid-single-digit comp sales growth and higher operating profit targets in 2026.
  • Strategic focus moved toward expanding Aerie and OFFLINE, with increased store openings and a clear exit from Quiet Logistics, compared to the prior quarter’s emphasis on marketing resets and inventory control.
  • Key metrics improved: Q4 revenue rose to $1.8 billion from $1.36 billion in Q3; adjusted operating income increased to $180 million from $113 million.
  • Management’s confidence was more pronounced, supported by strong holiday season performance and broad-based growth in all major brands.
  • Analysts' questions shifted from inventory and margin risk to longer-term growth drivers and operational efficiency, reflecting reduced near-term skepticism.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing tariff headwinds, with $30 million expected in Q1 and $130 million annually. The outlook does not incorporate potential changes from recent Supreme Court decisions.
  • Promotional intensity remains elevated in denim, though Aerie has been able to reduce markdowns.
  • Capital allocation priorities remain on business investment first, with share buybacks targeting dilution.
  • Store closures continue in lower productivity AE locations, with ongoing remodels to optimize the fleet.

Final Takeaway

American Eagle Outfitters closed fiscal 2025 with record revenue and robust operating income, propelled by strong performances at Aerie and OFFLINE and a disciplined focus on profitability and operational efficiency. The company projects mid-single-digit comp sales growth and operating profit between $390 million and $410 million in 2026, while increasing investments in advertising, expanding its store base for Aerie and OFFLINE, and exiting Quiet Logistics to strengthen its core brands. Management expresses confidence in building on recent momentum, maintaining strong cash flow, and driving long-term shareholder value, while remaining attentive to tariff risks and evolving consumer trends.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Webull aims to double premium subscribers and expand B2B platform in 2026 as AI drives platform growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Webull Corporation (BULL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Anthony Michael Denier, President & Director, highlighted "strong progress and returns for our first full year as a public company," emphasizing record revenue and operating profit margin improvement. Denier stated, "We recorded revenue of $571 million, representing 46% growth from 2024, driven by record trading volumes across all asset categories."
  • Denier reported that customer assets reached $24.6 billion, an 81% increase from 2024, and described the successful integration and impact of their AI assistant, Vega: "Vega currently assists 1.2 million global users each week with 10% of weekly active users deploying the tool to answer over 10 million questions since creation."
  • Denier noted, "Webull Premium, our subscription-based service for active traders and long-term investors, has reached 102,000 subscribers by year-end, surpassing the 100,000 target we set for ourselves," and outlined the goal to double the premium subscriber base in the coming year.
  • Denier emphasized global expansion: "We now have more than 760,000 funded accounts outside the U.S.," and detailed new market entries and licensing progress in Europe and APAC.
  • Denier stated, "Looking ahead to 2026... we have identified 3 main priorities for the year. First, we will sustain and grow our elite offerings for active traders... Second, we will continue growing our global business... Finally... we will be building on last year's partnership with Meritz to expand our B2B platform."
  • H. Wang, CFO & Director, reported, "In the fourth quarter, Webull generated total revenue of $165.2 million, representing 50% year-over-year growth. This strong performance reflects continued strength across both trading and interest-related income streams."

Outlook

  • Denier stated, "Looking ahead, we aim to double our premium subscriber base in the coming year while continuing to enhance the product with additional features, making it the best value product for active traders."
  • The company is "actively exploring digital asset licenses in a number of other markets and expect to bring them online in the coming year."
  • Denier said, "We are poised to bring our solutions to brokerage firms, high-net-individuals, family offices and wealth advisers," with B2B opportunities highlighted as a strategic focus in 2026.

Financial Results

  • Wang reported, "In the fourth quarter, Webull generated total revenue of $165.2 million, representing 50% year-over-year growth."
  • Adjusted operating expenses were $143.6 million, up 62% year-over-year, primarily driven by increased marketing and branding investments.
  • Adjusted operating profit was $21.6 million, representing a 13% adjusted operating profit margin, and adjusted net income was $14.6 million, or 8.8% of revenue.
  • Trading-related revenues increased 56% year-over-year to $112.5 million, supported by DARTs of 1,202 in the quarter.
  • Interest-related income grew 31% year-over-year to $43.5 million, driven by higher interest earned on client cash, margin lending, and corporate cash.
  • Customer margin balances increased 43% year-over-year to $689 million at the end of Q4.

Q&A

  • Christopher Brendler, Rosenblatt Securities Inc.: Asked about the marketing spend in Q4 and expectations going forward. Denier responded, "Q4 marketing expense was certainly higher... The majority of the marketing spend... is most impactful for the customers that we are focused on acquiring, and those are high-net-worth active trading customers." Denier added, "I do not expect that the marketing costs will be as high going forward... Q1 is looking much lighter than Q4 was."
  • Brendler also asked about Q1 2026 trends in DARTs and product mix. Denier replied, "January is probably the second best month we've ever had as a company since inception... Q1 is certainly looking strong."
  • Mike Grondahl, Northland Capital Markets: Inquired about the source of $3.9 billion net new deposits. Denier clarified, "Any of the B2B relationships that we've onboarded, they're not attributable to net deposits. Those net deposits are purely coming from retail. Crypto, however, is included because our crypto business is only attributed to retail right now."
  • Karim Assef, Bank of America: Asked about capital priorities and M&A. Wang answered, "We'll continue to be very focused investing in growth... primarily in organic growth as we see a lot of opportunities in our current space."
  • Edward Engel, Compass Point: Queried about performance marketing and growth drivers. Denier highlighted, "Our non-U.S. broker-dealers... have more than doubled the amount of incoming flow over the course of '25."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed positive sentiment, congratulating management on strong growth, but pressed for detail on marketing expenditures, B2B ramp-up, and the contribution of new products. Questions frequently focused on sustainability and clarity of strategy.
  • Management’s tone was confident during prepared remarks, emphasizing growth, innovation, and operational discipline. During Q&A, responses remained confident but included some hedging on the outlook for prediction markets and marketing spend, as indicated by Denier's "I do not believe that prediction markets are going to be any part of our core business going forward."
  • Compared to Q3, analyst tone remained positive but slightly more probing on growth initiatives and expense management. Management maintained a confident tone, though cautious regarding new business segments.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to more ambitious targets, including the stated aim to double premium subscribers in the next year.
  • Strategic priorities expanded to include a clear focus on B2B platform growth and further AI integration across the business.
  • Marketing spend increased sequentially, with management explaining the rise as a strategic, opportunistic push to capture customer assets during favorable market conditions. In Q3, marketing expenses were lower and focus was on ROI. In Q4, marketing as a percentage of revenue decreased from 35% in 2024 to about 23-24% in 2025.
  • International expansion accelerated, with new market entries and licensing detailed in the EU and APAC. Customer assets, trading volumes, and product adoption all showed sequential growth, but funded account additions slowed from 200,000 in Q3 to 100,000 in Q4.
  • Management tone remained optimistic, highlighting new AI and product launches, while analysts increased their scrutiny regarding the sustainability of growth and expense trends.

Risks and Concerns

  • Elevated marketing and branding spend was highlighted as a deliberate but temporary strategy, with management noting flexibility to adjust spend as necessary.
  • Wang acknowledged, "We will continue to obviously invest in customer acquisition and AUM growth, but we will also be keeping an eye on this ratio, percentage of revenue and spend on marketing."
  • Denier indicated the prediction markets are not expected to be a "core business" driver, signaling uncertainty about their long-term revenue impact.
  • Management cited the early stage of B2B partnerships, noting "the pipeline is primed and ready" but acknowledging longer ramp times and uncertain revenue contributions.

Final Takeaway

Webull Corporation closed its first full year as a public company with record revenue, robust expansion in both product offerings and international markets, and the successful integration of AI-driven features. The company aims to double its premium subscriber base and expand B2B operations in 2026, while maintaining disciplined investment in customer acquisition and technology. Management remains confident in the sustainability of growth, citing strong engagement with active traders and diversification across asset classes and geographies, but is cautious regarding the near-term impact of newer products like prediction markets and crypto. Investors can expect continued operational focus on scaling the platform and leveraging AI, while monitoring cost controls and evolving global opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gaotu Techedu GAAP EPADS of -$0.05, revenue of $240.99M beats by $3.09M; introduces Q1 outlook
data.description:
• Gaotu Techedu press release (GOTU): Q4 GAAP EPADS of -$0.05.
  • Non-GAAP basic and diluted net loss per ADS were both RMB0.32 in the fourth quarter of 2025.
  • Revenue of $240.99M (+21.4% Y/Y) beats by $3.09M.
  • Gross billings were RMB2,573.7 million, increased by 19.1% from RMB2,160.2 million in the same period of 2024.
  • Net operating cash inflow was RMB964.8 million, increased by 23.1% from RMB783.6 million in the same period of 2024.
  • Net operating cash inflow in the fourth quarter of 2025 was RMB964.8 million.

  • Business Outlook

    Based on the Company's current estimates, total net revenues for the first quarter of 2026 are expected to be between RMB1,578 million and RMB1,598 million, representing an increase of 5.7% to 7.0% on a year-over-year basis.

data.title: Carney: Canada will defend allies “when it makes sense” in Iran conflict
data.description:
• Prime Minister Mark Carney said Canada cannot yet rule out military involvement in the intensifying Middle East conflict.
  • Speaking to reporters at a joint news conference with Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese in Canberra, Carney said the question around Canada’s potential future involvement is a “fundamental hypothetical,”  adding the conflict can spread very broadly. 
  • When asked specifically whether that would remain the case should the hostilities expand or continue, the prime minister said, “One can never categorically rule out participation.” “We will stand by our allies when it makes sense.”

  • Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
